title: Beers and Weirs

jokes.text: Harold says 'Who am I trying to impress?' about wearing pajamas/shabby pants to breakfast
jokes.setup: Jean suggests Lindsay buy new clothes because her jeans look ragged
jokes.punchline: Harold deflects by pointing out he himself dresses sloppily at home, rhetorical 'who am I trying to impress?' — self-unaware dad comfort
jokes.explanation: Efficient Harold characterization — the oblivious dad who holds others to standards he ignores. Functional opener, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Harold: 'When it's your house, you can wear a tuxedo to breakfast.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay pushes back on being told to buy new clothes while Harold wears raggedy clothes
jokes.punchline: Harold's non-sequitur parental authority line — tuxedo as the absurd extreme — delivered with total conviction
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Harold Weir dad-logic: internally consistent, delivered with zero self-awareness. The tuxedo specificity elevates it above a generic 'my house my rules' line. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Jean concedes kids are too old for a babysitter, then immediately adds: 'Mrs. Amendella makes the house smell like an old lady anyway.'
jokes.setup: Harold and Jean debate whether the kids need a babysitter; Jean reluctantly agrees they're old enough
jokes.punchline: Jean's real objection wasn't the kids' maturity — it was the smell. The logic inversion reveals her actual priorities hilariously.
jokes.explanation: Quick, well-timed subversion: the parental capitulation is actually driven by petty sensory complaint, not genuine confidence in the kids. Economical and true to suburban mom logic.

jokes.text: Harold: 'You know those Sex Pistols? They spit on their audience. That's what I want to do. Spend my hard-earned money to be spit on. Now THAT'S entertainment.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay defends punk rock by saying every generation fears the next generation's music
jokes.punchline: Harold's hyper-literal complaint about the spitting — delivered with maximum indignation as if it's the singular reason punk is unacceptable
jokes.explanation: Harold reducing his entire critique of punk to the economic insult of paying to be spat on is perfectly him. 'Now THAT'S entertainment' with sarcastic italics is solid delivery. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'No, but he died on the toilet.' — in response to Harold defending Elvis over the Sex Pistols
jokes.setup: Harold argues Elvis is superior because 'Elvis didn't expectorate on his fans' — using the word 'expectorate' as a mark of his sophistication
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's blunt, factually accurate counter: Elvis's toilet death undercuts any dignified comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — six words demolish Harold's entire argument. The contrast between Harold's $10 word 'expectorate' and Lindsay's blunt 'died on the toilet' is structurally sharp. One of the best exchanges in the cold open.

jokes.text: Harold: 'Well, that's paradise compared to where those Sex Pistols are gonna end up.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay points out Elvis died on the toilet as a counterpoint to Harold's praise
jokes.punchline: Harold doubles down — the toilet death is fine, Sex Pistols are going somewhere worse — he can't concede the argument
jokes.explanation: Good escalation beat — Harold refuses to lose the argument even when Lindsay has a valid point. 'That's paradise compared to...' is a solid callback structure.

jokes.text: Nick is devastated about John Bonham's death. Lindsay asks: 'Well, why don't they just get a new drummer?' Nick is speechless.
jokes.setup: Nick is visibly grief-stricken about Bonham's death and the end of Led Zeppelin
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's innocent, practical suggestion — get a new drummer — is so tone-deaf to rock reverence that Nick can only stare, then say 'Let's just forget it'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully observed: Lindsay hasn't fully absorbed her new freaks identity, so she applies reasonable-person logic to something that is sacred to a rock devotee. Nick's stunned silence IS the joke. Strong character integration for both — her outsider status and his religious devotion to music are both revealed.

jokes.text: Daniel explains his elaborate price-tag-switching shoplifting scheme. Lindsay: 'Oh, it's beautiful. Clearly worth all the effort.'
jokes.setup: Daniel explains a convoluted multi-step scheme to essentially steal a jacket for $10
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's dry response — 'clearly worth all the effort' — is devastating sarcasm. The scheme is absurdly complicated for minimal gain.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's dry wit lands well here — she's smart enough to see through Daniel's petty criminal pride. Good character contrast.

jokes.text: Ken: 'Hey, if I owned your store, I caught some little kid shoplifting, I'd just take him out back and deal with it.' Kim: 'Oh, would you, Mr. Tough Man?' Ken: 'Yeah, I would, Mrs. Tough Man.'
jokes.setup: Ken postures as a hard man who'd handle shoplifters his own way
jokes.punchline: Kim's sarcastic 'Mr. Tough Man' immediately deflates him; Ken's comeback — 'Mrs. Tough Man' — is so lame it's funny
jokes.explanation: 'Mrs. Tough Man' is a deliberately weak comeback that reveals Ken's bluster — he's all talk. Functional character beat.

jokes.text: Kim immediately embarrasses Ken: 'Oh, like that time when John Ergner kicked your ass? Like — like that?'
jokes.setup: Ken has just boasted he'd 'deal with' shoplifters out back
jokes.punchline: Kim has a specific, ready story about Ken being beaten up, completely destroying his tough guy persona
jokes.explanation: Kim's specific recall of the Ergner incident is funnier than a generic deflation — the detail makes it feel like a real, humiliating history.

jokes.text: Lindsay agrees to the party just to prove she's not lame. Kim: 'Great. Who's the too-lame one?' Kim sighs: 'Up yours.'
jokes.setup: Kim calls Lindsay too lame to let anyone in her house, assuming she'll refuse
jokes.punchline: Lindsay calls Kim's bluff, agreeing to the party; Kim wins but is then annoyed — the reversal leaves Kim with nowhere to go except 'up yours'
jokes.explanation: The comedic satisfaction of Kim being outmaneuvered by her own manipulation is well-structured. Kim's 'up yours' after losing the argument is a perfect sulky beat.

jokes.text: Neal's 'Scared Straight' logic chain: 'If you don't care about high school, you won't get into a good college, you'll have no future, and you'll wind up dead or in jail.' Sam: 'Really?' Neal: 'Were you asleep during Scared Straight?'
jokes.setup: Neal watches the freaks from afar, worried about Lindsay's future
jokes.punchline: Neal's catastrophizing logic is so extreme it becomes absurd, and his indignant 'Were you asleep during Scared Straight?' treats the documentary as essential curriculum
jokes.explanation: Neal's anxious, over-educated reasoning — zero to 'dead or in jail' in one breath — is a great geek character beat. 'Were you asleep during Scared Straight?' is genuinely funny as a rhetorical gotcha.

jokes.text: Sam: 'You're in love with Lindsay.' Neal vehemently denies it. Sam: 'Because you have your book in front of your pants.'
jokes.setup: Neal's passionate concern about Lindsay's future prompts Sam to accuse him of being in love with her
jokes.punchline: Sam's evidence — Neal is holding a book in front of his crotch — is both hilarious and mortifyingly specific
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect structure: Neal's overblown denial only makes the punchline land harder. The specificity of 'in front of your pants' as hard adolescent evidence is both funny and sweetly true to teenage experience. Strong economy — Sam delivers the observation in six words. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Sam negotiates the party secret and worries: 'Dad will yell at me for not telling on you.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay tells Sam he can't tell their parents about the party
jokes.punchline: Sam's concern isn't being caught — it's being punished for the specific offense of failing to snitch. The bureaucratic logic of sibling complicity is perfectly captured.
jokes.explanation: Sam's rule-following anxiety is well-observed. The joke is in his framing: he's not worried about the party, he's worried about the procedural consequences of silence.

jokes.text: Anti-drinking improv assembly: student actor sobs 'I didn't want to hurt anyone' after being compared to a loaded gun
jokes.setup: The designated driver gives a stilted anti-drunk-driving speech to a drunk student in the improv skit
jokes.punchline: The drunk student's overwrought sobbing at the 'loaded gun' metaphor is an absurdly melodramatic response that deflates the sincere educational intent
jokes.explanation: The school anti-drinking assembly improv is a rich vein of cringe comedy — the overwrought student performance is funny precisely because everyone in the room knows it's bad.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso: 'Maybe if you DON'T drink, you WILL be cool.' — delivered with earnest confidence
jokes.setup: Rosso anticipates students' 'I won't be cool if I don't drink' objection
jokes.punchline: His reversal — that sobriety might itself be cool — is delivered with the confidence of someone who has just cracked a logical breakthrough, unaware of how absurd it sounds
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mr. Rosso moment — the well-meaning hippie counselor thinks he's being subversive and cool while delivering the squarest possible message.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso asks the room for 'a kind of party where drinking might occur.' Student immediately yells: 'A SEX PARTY.'
jokes.setup: Rosso invites improv suggestions for a party scenario
jokes.punchline: The student's immediate 'sex party' answer — and Rosso's rapid pivot to 'I think I heard someone say birthday party' — is a sharp bit of institutional comedy
jokes.explanation: Classic subversion: the only student who takes Rosso up on his 'open' invitation immediately weaponizes it. Rosso's smooth 'birthday party' redirect is the funnier half — bureaucratic denial in real time. Big room laugh moment.

jokes.text: Harris in the birthday party improv casually asks: 'You know if anyone here has any cocaine?'
jokes.setup: The improv sketch is supposed to demonstrate sober birthday party fun
jokes.punchline: Harris goes completely off-script asking for cocaine at what is supposed to be an anti-drug demonstration
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of a cocaine request inside an anti-drug improv is a great escalation of the assembly scene's absurdity. Harris asking like it's normal party small talk is the key.

jokes.text: In the improv, two students sneak off during the party: 'I'm gonna go. Count to 10, then come.' Harris, alone, announces: 'This is the best birthday ever.'
jokes.setup: The birthday party skit is supposed to show wholesome fun; two performers conspicuously escape
jokes.punchline: Harris's 'this is the best birthday ever' while visibly abandoned is simultaneously tragic and funny — the skit has collapsed entirely
jokes.explanation: A good visual absurdist beat — the earnest pro-sobriety skit has devolved into a cocaine request, two escapees, and one kid alone saying it's the best birthday ever.

jokes.text: Rosso concludes the wreckage: 'Nothing is more contagious than good judgment.'
jokes.setup: The improv skit has been a complete disaster — cocaine, sneaking off, abandonment
jokes.punchline: Rosso's cheery summary — 'nothing is more contagious than good judgment' — is obliviously tone-deaf to the chaos that just occurred
jokes.explanation: The gap between what just happened and Rosso's breezy spin is the joke. The phrase itself is also intrinsically funny — a slogan so earnest it lands as unintentional satire.

jokes.text: Millie: 'Let's go get a glass of pop. I hope they have orange.' / Harris: 'Hey, it's a party, isn't it?'
jokes.setup: The improv sketch is modeling sober fun at a party
jokes.punchline: The height of sober fun being 'orange pop,' and Harris's big 'hey, it's a party!' energy in response to this being the highlight
jokes.explanation: The deflation of 'sober fun' into specific, mundane desires (orange pop) undercuts the whole premise of the assembly perfectly. 'Hey, it's a party, isn't it?' works as existentially sad comedy.

jokes.text: Ken: 'I love being told not to drink by a pothead hippie guidance counselor. There's probably a bar in the teacher's lounge.'
jokes.setup: Rosso is using dead teens as cautionary examples against drinking
jokes.punchline: Ken's cynical commentary cuts through the assembly's hypocrisy — and the specificity of 'bar in the teacher's lounge' is a good detail
jokes.explanation: Ken's role as the cynical commentator is consistent and well-deployed here. The observation isn't groundbreaking but it's well-integrated character comedy.

jokes.text: Neal: 'What? Bill, Dallas sucks.' Bill: 'You suck. Dallas rules.'
jokes.setup: Neal challenges Bill's Dallas priorities
jokes.punchline: Bill's passionate, unironic defense of Dallas — 'Dallas rules' — reveals his earnest investment in prime-time soap opera
jokes.explanation: Short escalation of the Dallas bit. Bill saying 'you suck' in genuine outrage about a TV show opinion is good character work.

jokes.text: Bill capitulates to going — 'Fine, I'll go. But I'm watching Dallas there.' Then, to the girls who overheard: 'I was just joking. I don't — I don't watch Dallas.'
jokes.setup: Bill agrees to go but insists on watching Dallas at the party; girls laugh at him
jokes.punchline: Bill's immediate social shame rollback — 'I was just joking, I don't watch Dallas' — is the joke: he's lying to save face and everyone knows it
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the Dallas running gag: Bill's social self-consciousness overrides his genuine passion. The escalation from 'it's on' → 'Dallas rules' → desperate denial in front of girls is a small, perfect character arc within one scene.

jokes.text: Harold's list of household instructions escalates to: 'Don't blow the $50 on those Star Wars cards or those goofy packs.' Sam: 'Wacky Packs.' Harold: 'Ah, whatever.'
jokes.setup: Harold is giving emergency instructions before leaving for Chicago
jokes.punchline: Harold knows Sam's trading card obsession well enough to specifically prohibit it, but not well enough to name the product correctly — and doesn't care to be corrected
jokes.explanation: Harold's approximate parenting — he knows enough to be specific, not enough to be right — is a good character beat. 'Ah, whatever' dismissing Sam's correction is perfectly Harold.

jokes.text: Harold's final departure instruction: 'And turn out the lights when you go out. I'm not made of money.'
jokes.setup: Harold is delivering a long list of instructions; this comes after gas company, $50 on the table, no trading cards
jokes.punchline: The anti-climactic final instruction — lights out, I'm not made of money — deflates the drama of leaving the kids alone for the first time
jokes.explanation: Functional Harold character beat — the dad who undercuts emotional moments with household economics. Not exceptional but consistent.

jokes.text: Sam: 'One of their moms talked. She was pretty sad. It kind of freaked everybody out.' Then: 'Oh, and at assembly today, it said that if you drink too much you can die of alcohol poisoning.' Lindsay: 'Sam, you sound like Dad.'
jokes.setup: Sam has been cataloguing every sobering thing from the anti-drinking assembly
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's devastatingly accurate 'you sound like Dad' — the ultimate dismissal from a teenager to a sibling
jokes.explanation: The running sam-as-mini-Harold joke is well-deployed here. The callback to Harold's paranoid parenting style without Harold present.

jokes.text: Sam: 'Well, remember the party where you had a magician? That was fun. Why don't you have a magician?' Lindsay: 'We are not having a magi-- WHY AM I TALKING TO YOU?'
jokes.setup: Sam is trying to talk Lindsay out of serving beer at her party, suggesting alternatives
jokes.punchline: Sam's completely sincere magician suggestion — applied to a high school house party — is so tone-deaf to the situation that Lindsay breaks entirely
jokes.explanation: Sam's genuine innocence about what a high school party is supposed to be — his reference point is childhood birthday parties — is perfectly observed. Lindsay starting to argue then catching herself ('WHY AM I TALKING TO YOU') is a great structure.

jokes.text: The freaks use a fake 'emergency phone call from mom' to pull Lindsay out of class. Sara, the other student: 'No problem. See you at the party tonight. That's a cute necklace.'
jokes.setup: Kim sends a student to pull Lindsay out of calculus with a fake family emergency
jokes.punchline: Sara casually blowing the cover by referencing 'the party tonight' to Lindsay's face — and then offering a compliment about her necklace as if that makes it fine
jokes.explanation: The social obliviousness of Sara's cover-blowing combined with the non-sequitur necklace compliment is a good brief comedy beat.

jokes.text: Sam's plan to stop the party: hit Lindsay on the head so she has a bump, then call the parents, then blame the bump on the party.
jokes.setup: The geeks are trying to figure out how to cancel the party
jokes.punchline: Their escalating plan — fake emergency → actually hit her → frame the bump — reveals how deeply they've failed to think any of this through
jokes.explanation: The Dirty Harry movie reference they use to explain the plan adds a layer — they've learned their problem-solving from films. Functional geek absurdist comedy.

jokes.text: The geeks' plan to cancel the party: 'Maybe you can call your parents and tell them you fell and hit your head.' 'Then what happens when they come home and see my head is fine?' 'Well, we'd have to hit you over the head and give you a bump.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are trying to find a way to cancel the party before it starts.
jokes.punchline: The plan escalates from a white lie to 'we'd physically injure you' with complete logical sincerity. No one in the room finds this alarming.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the logical chain: lie → parents come home → head looks fine → solution: actually injure friend. The escalation is perfectly calibrated. The geeks treat this as a reasonable problem-solving session. Strong character work — this is exactly how 14-year-old boys scheme.

jokes.text: Neal explains non-alcoholic beer: Bill asks 'What's non-alcoholic beer?' Neal: 'It's just like beer, but it doesn't have that ingredient that makes you drunk.' Bill: 'Alcohol?' Neal: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Neal suggests switching to non-alcoholic beer to save the party
jokes.punchline: Bill's slow-processing clarification question — 'Alcohol?' — stated as if it's a genuine contribution, and Neal's flat 'Yeah' confirm Bill's magnificent obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Beautiful economy. The entire joke is two words — 'Alcohol?' and 'Yeah.' Neal's explanation builds the setup, but the payoff is Bill asking for the one obvious word that answers the question, stated as if he figured something out. Perfect Bill characterization.

jokes.text: Neal resists spending his bar mitzvah money: 'It's not like I get to have another one.'
jokes.setup: Sam and Bill pressure Neal to spend his bar mitzvah savings on non-alcoholic beer
jokes.punchline: Neal's grievance — you only get one bar mitzvah — is absolutely true but delivered with the tone of someone who has thought about this grievance many times
jokes.explanation: The logic is airtight — you genuinely only get one bar mitzvah — which makes it funnier. Neal's wounded specificity about this particular pot of money is strong character integration.

jokes.text: Neal: 'I'm saving it to go backpacking across Europe.'
jokes.setup: Neal is explaining why he can't spend his bar mitzvah money on NA beer
jokes.punchline: A 13-year-old's detailed plan to backpack Europe — stored in bar mitzvah savings — is an absurdly adult aspiration delivered completely earnestly
jokes.explanation: Neal's precocious adult plans are a consistent character note. The Europe backpacking aspiration among eighth-graders is sweetly incongruous.

jokes.text: Sam says 'Mazel tov' after Neal capitulates and agrees to spend his bar mitzvah money. Neal: 'Oh, shut up.'
jokes.setup: Neal reluctantly agrees to spend his sacred bar mitzvah money
jokes.punchline: Sam's 'Mazel tov' — congratulating Neal on giving up his bar mitzvah money by invoking his own cultural milestone — is a quick, precise callback
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect economy — one word, maximum irony. Using 'Mazel tov' to mock Neal for spending his bar mitzvah savings is a tight callback that earns its laugh in two syllables. Neal's 'oh, shut up' is the correct response.

jokes.text: Bill in the liquor store: 'What if somebody robs this place while we're in here? People are always getting shot in liquor stores.' Beat. 'Do you think they sell Yoo-hoo here? I'm kinda thirsty.'
jokes.setup: The geeks nervously enter a liquor store for the first time
jokes.punchline: Bill escalates from existential fear of being shot to an immediate, mundane refreshment request in one breath — his priorities reset instantly
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structural whiplash — genuine fear of violent death → 'do you think they sell Yoo-hoo' — is executed perfectly. The Yoo-hoo specificity is crucial; a lesser joke would say 'soda.' Bill's character is fully present: anxious, oblivious, and governed by appetite. Comparable in quality to an 8-range anchor example.

jokes.text: Neal to Bill after the Yoo-hoo comment: 'Yeah, Bill, why don't you ask the guy? Maybe he can change your diaper, too.' Bill: 'Hey, that was unnecessary.'
jokes.setup: Bill asks about Yoo-hoo in a liquor store while they're trying to complete a covert mission
jokes.punchline: Neal's diaper insult is harsh; Bill's wounded 'that was unnecessary' is the real punchline — utterly dignified in response to being compared to an infant
jokes.explanation: Bill's 'that was unnecessary' is the funnier half — he takes the moral high ground after asking about Yoo-hoo in a liquor store. Good character beat.

jokes.text: Sam to the liquor store clerk, deadpan: 'We're out of Pop Rocks. Uh, a keg of beer, please.' Clerk looks at them. 'Uh, no, we'd like non-alcoholic beer.'
jokes.setup: Three obviously underage boys approach a liquor store counter
jokes.punchline: Sam's attempt to order casually — 'a keg of beer, please' — is so helplessly formal and childlike that the 'please' alone makes it funny; the retreat to NA beer is the second beat
jokes.explanation: The 'please' when ordering a keg as a minor is perfectly observed adolescent formality. The 'we're out of Pop Rocks' non-sequitur suggests they had a strategy that evaporated under pressure.

jokes.text: Sam explains the keg switch to the clerk: 'Oh, we're gonna switch the kegs at his sister's party.' Clerk laughs: 'Righteous.'
jokes.setup: The clerk asks what the NA beer is for
jokes.punchline: Sam has no guile — he immediately tells the clerk the entire plan. The clerk's approving 'Righteous' is the adult endorsing teenage scheming
jokes.explanation: Sam's total lack of deception is a great character beat — he confesses the whole plan to the one adult who could stop them, and gets an enthusiastic response. The clerk's 'Righteous' is perfectly period-specific and warmly absurd.

jokes.text: Neal tries to distract Lindsay by suggesting she play Chicago with their 'really hot horn section' while Sam and Bill struggle noisily with the keg outside
jokes.setup: Neal is supposed to keep Lindsay occupied while Sam and Bill secretly switch the kegs
jokes.punchline: Neal's distraction — recommending Chicago for their horn section — is so stilted and random that it barely holds Lindsay's attention while crashing noises come from outside
jokes.explanation: The Chicago horn section suggestion is perfectly wrong — the most earnest, least cool music recommendation — while thumping noises betray the operation outside. Classic cringe-distraction comedy.

jokes.text: Neal, while distracting Lindsay: 'Friday night, always a good night for some Sabbath. 'Cause you know, Friday... is the Sabbath... for the Jews.'
jokes.setup: Neal recommends Black Sabbath to Lindsay while trying to seem cool and distract her
jokes.punchline: Neal's literal religious etymology of 'Sabbath' — filling the silence desperately — is excruciating and funny. He's trying to be cool and ends up being an encyclopedia.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully specific cringe moment: Neal trying to seem like a cool Sabbath fan pivots into a nervous Talmudic aside about the calendar. The ellipses in the delivery — 'because you know, Friday... is the Sabbath... for the Jews' — capture the horrible dawning realization mid-sentence. Strong character integration: only Neal delivers this joke.

jokes.text: Bill revealing Neal's crush mid-keg-switch: 'Why? He's in love with your sister.' / Sam: 'What? Ow!'
jokes.setup: Sam asks why Neal cares so much about staying with Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Bill blurts out Neal's feelings at the worst possible moment — during a covert operation where they need to be quiet — and Sam's 'Ow!' suggests he was hit trying to shut Bill up
jokes.explanation: Bill's inability to keep secrets is funny; the 'Ow!' from Sam is a nice physical comedy inference.

jokes.text: Bill, still working on the keg switch while Neal is upstairs: 'If Neal marries your sister, then he'd be your brother-in-law. Then, if you had kids, Neal would be their Uncle Neal. And then I bet Lindsay's kids would look like Neal.'
jokes.setup: Sam and Bill are supposed to be covertly switching kegs. Bill begins speculating on Neal and Lindsay's hypothetical romantic future.
jokes.punchline: Bill's unprompted genealogical speculation — particularly 'I bet Lindsay's kids would look like Neal' as a bad outcome — derails the mission entirely
jokes.explanation: Bill's tangential, sustained speculation about Neal and Lindsay's theoretical children — delivered while committing a covert operation — is a perfect Bill moment. The implication that 'kids who look like Neal' is a bad outcome is an understated but funny dig.

jokes.text: Bill starts to reveal Neal told him something about Lindsay. Sam: 'He doesn't love Lindsay.' Bill: 'That's not what he told... me.' [BANG — something falls]
jokes.setup: Sam is insisting Neal doesn't love Lindsay; Bill starts to contradict him
jokes.punchline: Bill's revelation is interrupted by a crash — whether from the keg operation or something else — perfectly cutting off the embarrassing information
jokes.explanation: Classic interrupted revelation — the dramatic pause before 'me' followed by a physical crash is a well-executed sitcom structure. The crash serves double comic duty: cutting off Bill and explaining the noise to Lindsay.

jokes.text: Sam explains the crash to Lindsay: 'I think Bill's got an ear infection. It's messing with his equilibrium.'
jokes.setup: A loud bang comes from outside after Bill drops something during the keg switch
jokes.punchline: Sam's cover story — a specific medical condition affecting Bill's balance — is completely implausible but delivered with confidence
jokes.explanation: The medical specificity of 'ear infection affecting his equilibrium' as a cover story is a good geek-kid detail — Sam's instinct is to construct a logical-sounding lie.

jokes.text: Lindsay catches Bill — apparently having fallen into something or gotten undressed mid-keg-switch. Neal: 'Uh, Bill's got an ear infection.' Sam: 'Well, help him, Sam. Give him something to wear.'
jokes.setup: The keg switch has gone catastrophically wrong; Bill has been caught in a compromising state
jokes.punchline: The scramble — Neal invoking the ear infection cover story again, Sam being told to find Bill clothes — implies a chaotic physical situation we only hear about through dialogue
jokes.explanation: The ear infection callback earns a second laugh here. The physical situation is funnier on screen than in transcript — a running gag payoff via implied visual chaos.

jokes.text: Nick arrives at Lindsay's decorated house and says: 'What, are you... what do you think we are, hippies?' Then admires a unicorn: 'Wow. Like that unicorn.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has decorated her house for the party with what are apparently overly whimsical/hippie decorations
jokes.punchline: Nick's mock-complaint about the hippie aesthetic, followed by his genuine delight about the unicorn — the contradiction reveals he actually likes it
jokes.explanation: The inversion — Nick scoffs then immediately expresses sincere unicorn appreciation — is a quick, charming character beat.

jokes.text: Daniel arrives, immediately asks to be pointed to the keg: 'Well, I'll tell you how to make it look more party-like. Point me to the keg.' Nick: 'Oh yeah.' Daniel: 'Then I am in the corner.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is explaining she decorated to make the house more 'party-like'
jokes.punchline: Daniel's uninterested redirect — his only metric for party quality is keg location — followed by 'then I am in the corner,' delivered like a man going to his destiny
jokes.explanation: 'Then I am in the corner' — said with full self-awareness and zero self-consciousness — is a great Daniel line. Economical and entirely in character. He has no interest in Lindsay's effort; his only concern is the keg's coordinates.

jokes.text: A partygoer: 'This beer sucks. It taste weird to you?' Daniel: 'Uh, it's imported. Nothing's too good for our friends.'
jokes.setup: The NA beer is in the keg; a partygoer notices it tastes off
jokes.punchline: Daniel covers by claiming it's imported — a completely bogus explanation he delivers with total confidence, and the absurd 'nothing's too good for our friends' adds a generous host veneer
jokes.explanation: 'Nothing's too good for our friends' as a fake hospitality line to cover a deception is a sharp Daniel character beat. He's smooth under pressure.

jokes.text: Another partygoer: 'Daniel probably bought the cheap beer and kept the extra cash.' Another: 'Definitely.'
jokes.setup: The NA beer tastes wrong; a partygoer has their own theory
jokes.punchline: The partygoers' alternate explanation — Daniel simply pocketed the money — is actually more likely than the truth, and the 'Definitely' is knowingly resigned
jokes.explanation: A brief comedic beat that lands because Daniel's reputation makes the false accusation plausible — and it inadvertently protects the actual scheme.

jokes.text: Nick's elaborate reading of Led Zeppelin symbolism — 'in Song Remains the Same, he's dressed like a gangster, his head got shot off and multi-colored paint came out of his neck' — and his conclusion that Bonham 'knew he was gonna die.' Lindsay interrupts: 'Could you not sit on that, please? It's glass.'
jokes.setup: Nick is elaborating a grandiose theory about John Bonham's symbolic foreknowledge of his own death
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's entirely mundane interruption — protecting glass furniture — deflates Nick's cosmic rock theorizing completely
jokes.explanation: The comedy lives in the gap between Nick's profundity and Lindsay's domestic interruption. Well-structured: we need Nick to be fully invested for the deflation to work.

jokes.text: Neal, talking to a cool older guy: 'I don't know. I mean, I'm Jewish. It's no cake-walk either.' Then: 'I was elected school treasurer last year. I didn't even run.'
jokes.setup: Someone is complaining about something hard about their life; Neal tries to bond by comparing
jokes.punchline: Neal's contribution to the conversation about hardship is being involuntarily elected treasurer — offered as equivalent suffering, delivered with absolute sincerity
jokes.explanation: The two-beat structure is strong: first Neal plays the Jewish identity card as social currency, then escalates to the involuntary treasurer election as evidence of his particular burden. 'I didn't even run' as the detail that makes it tragic-funny is perfectly Neal — he is so obviously competent that he gets elected to things against his will. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Millie confronts Lindsay at the party: 'Were you skipping out with your new friends? Or should I call them... drinking buddies?'
jokes.setup: Millie appears at the party, visibly disapproving
jokes.punchline: Millie's pointed pause before 'drinking buddies' — delivered like she's rehearsed the line — reveals her passive-aggressive Christian-girl wounded dignity
jokes.explanation: Millie's performative moral concern is a good character beat. The rehearsed quality of 'drinking buddies' is the tell.

jokes.text: Millie is offered beer. Millie: 'No, thank you. I prefer to get high on life.'
jokes.setup: Someone offers Millie a beer at the party
jokes.punchline: Millie's 'high on life' response is a sanctimonious cliché delivered completely unironically — making it both funny and endearing
jokes.explanation: The line is a cliché, but Millie's sincerity is the character. She means it completely, which is both funny and oddly sympathetic.

jokes.text: Millie's departing declaration: 'And I'm going to have more fun than any of you. Sober.' [tires screech as she drives off]
jokes.setup: Millie has been pressuring Lindsay to leave and Lindsay refuses
jokes.punchline: Millie announcing she'll have more fun than everyone at the party — sober — then peeling out in a car as if leaving dramatically from a confrontation, is a perfectly absurd Millie exit
jokes.explanation: The screeching tires after 'I'm going to have more fun than any of you. Sober.' is a perfect visual comedy exclamation point. The gap between Millie's wholesome self-righteousness and the aggressive driving exit is a great tonal collision.

jokes.text: Daniel's older friends arrive. Kim: 'Where's your boy?' Friend: 'Hopefully under a truck. I dumped his ass.'
jokes.setup: Daniel's older, tougher-looking friends arrive at the party and ask about someone
jokes.punchline: Kim's 'hopefully under a truck' is casual, specific, and delivered without drama — the throwaway violence of it is funny precisely because of its matter-of-fact delivery
jokes.explanation: Kim's violence-as-small-talk is a sharp character beat. Economy is high — four words to convey her contempt, his fate, and her complete indifference.

jokes.text: Older guy to Kim: 'That makes you available.' Kim: 'No way, old man. She's mine.' 'AAH! AAH! ALL MINE.' [laughter]
jokes.setup: Older guys flirt with Kim; Kim rejects with violence
jokes.punchline: Kim's physically aggressive rejection — apparently grabbing/attacking the guy — and her 'all mine' possessiveness over Lindsay is chaotic and funny
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy. Kim's possessiveness of Lindsay as expressed through violence toward someone else is an interesting character note.

jokes.text: Older guy at the party: [belches]. 'Oh, man, I gotta pee!'
jokes.setup: The NA beer is working — partygoers think they're drunk and are behaving accordingly
jokes.punchline: The guy's belch immediately followed by urgent urination need — entirely from non-alcoholic beer — is the placebo effect in crude action
jokes.explanation: Crude but functional — serves the NA beer scheme plot. Not particularly clever, but contextually appropriate.

jokes.text: One guy: 'Man, I've had 5 beers already. I'm not even buzzed.' Another guy: 'You're the man! I don't know what to say. You're the man!'
jokes.setup: The NA beer scheme is working; partygoers have no idea why they're not drunk
jokes.punchline: The guy's complaint that he can't get drunk is met with fulsome admiration — his 'tolerance' is celebrated while the audience knows he's drinking water essentially
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony of his 'tolerance' being celebrated is the joke. Competent.

jokes.text: Millie, sober, announces: 'I haven't had any and I'm sailing.'
jokes.setup: A partygoer can't get buzzed on 5 beers; Millie has had none
jokes.punchline: Millie announcing she's 'sailing' without drinking anything — experiencing a placebo placebo effect, or just enthusiasm — delivers on her earlier promise to have more fun sober
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback and character payoff: Millie actually IS having the time of her life at a party where everyone drinks NA beer. She's the only one whose experience is completely authentic — and it's identical to the 'drunk' people. The joke rewards her earlier promise and the NA beer scheme simultaneously. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Harris, the 'Sober Students' performer: 'I only did that because it looks good on my transcript for college.'
jokes.setup: Someone recognizes Harris as a Sober Student from the assembly
jokes.punchline: Harris immediately confesses his performance was entirely cynical — 'it looks good on my transcript' — while at a keg party
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff. The assembly's earnest anti-drinking messaging is revealed to be entirely transactional from the inside — Harris wasn't even slightly committed to sobriety. The specificity ('looks good on my transcript for college') is very 1980 and very true. Strong economy. Justifies the near-8 score.

jokes.text: The 'sober student' from the improv is at the party drinking. Sam confronts him: 'Aren't you a sober student?' He replies: 'I only did that because it looks good on my transcript for college.'
jokes.setup: Sam recognizes the student who played the Designated Driver in the anti-drinking improv from the assembly
jokes.punchline: The designated driver performed sobriety purely as a college application strategy — the entire anti-drinking assembly is revealed as a cynical transaction
jokes.explanation: A great callback that retroactively undercuts the entire assembly sequence. The 'transcript for college' motivation is specifically cynical in a way that feels true. Good structural payoff to a running thread.

jokes.text: A guy at the party is inviting others to punch him in the stomach: 'Hit me harder. Harder, man, come on! Feel the steel! Man, you're tickling me.'
jokes.setup: The placebo drunk partygoers are emboldened; one demonstrates his 'toughness'
jokes.punchline: A guy challenging people to hit him — and accusing them of tickling — is the kind of faux-macho posturing that only happens under (perceived) intoxication
jokes.explanation: Functional party chaos moment. Not particularly crafted but serves the 'placebo drunk' visual comedy.

jokes.text: Sam and Neal observe the chaos. Neal: 'They really think they're drunk.' Sam: 'Think so?' Neal: 'Look at them. They're wasted. That's the placebo effect working for you, my friend.'
jokes.setup: The geeks watch their NA beer scheme succeed beyond expectations
jokes.punchline: Neal explaining the placebo effect to Sam while watching grown adults act drunk on root beer is delivered with the satisfaction of a scientist observing his experiment
jokes.explanation: Neal naming the placebo effect is a good character beat — he's the geek who has a scientific framework for everything, including accidental social experiments.

jokes.text: Daniel looks at Lindsay's childhood trophies: 'First place — Algebra Division?' Then: 'Oh, that's right. You're one of those, uh... Mathletes.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is nosing around Lindsay's room and finds her academic trophies
jokes.punchline: Daniel's 'one of those' — with a pause before 'Mathletes' as if retrieving the word — is delivered with casual, unintentional condescension
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'Mathletes' does the work — Daniel isn't even mean about it, just categorizing. His following 'Hey, if I ever won a blue ribbon, I'd be so pumped' is the more interesting beat.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'Hey, if I ever won a blue ribbon... I'd be so pumped.' Beat. 'I mean, uh, I wouldn't wear it around or nothing, but... it's cool.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has been dismissing Lindsay's Mathlete trophy as 'stupid'; Lindsay defends it
jokes.punchline: Daniel's earnest admission — he'd be pumped to win anything — followed by the hasty qualification 'I wouldn't wear it around or nothing' is a rare moment of vulnerability patched over with cool-guy damage control
jokes.explanation: This is Freaks and Geeks at its best: a comedy moment that is also a genuine character revelation. Daniel's macho retreat from his own sincerity — 'I wouldn't wear it around or nothing' — is funnier than any joke because it's true. The comedy comes from watching him almost open up and then slam the door. High character integration: this is a Daniel-only moment.

jokes.text: Partygoer recognizes Millie from the anti-drinking assembly: 'Oh. Oh, no! That's that chick from the assembly, man.'
jokes.setup: Millie has shown up at the party and is singing a Christian song
jokes.punchline: A drunk-acting partygoer recognizing Millie from her designated driver performance earlier in the day — in this context — is a good callback beat
jokes.explanation: A functional callback that bridges the assembly scenes with the party, adding irony to Millie's presence.

jokes.text: Millie sings a Jesus song loudly at the party while drunk-acting partygoers are around her
jokes.setup: Millie is 'sailing' at the party sober; the partygoers are around her
jokes.punchline: Millie's complete unselfconsciousness in singing an earnest Christian song at a teen house party is a sustained visual cringe-comedy moment
jokes.explanation: Millie's joyful obliviousness is the engine of her comedy — she genuinely doesn't register the incongruity of her behavior in context. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Sam approaches Ken to ask him to look out for Lindsay. Ken: 'OK.' Sam: 'Thanks.' Ken's friend: 'Hey, which one's your sister? She's not that chick who's singing, is she?'
jokes.setup: Sam asks Ken to protect Lindsay at the party; Ken coolly agrees
jokes.punchline: Ken's friend's immediate concern that Lindsay might be the singing Christian girl — which would be an enormous red flag — is the punchline
jokes.explanation: Well-structured: the cool guy's one concern is whether the girl he's supposed to protect is the religious singer, which is both funny and a fair thing to want to clarify.

jokes.text: Two guys start fighting at the party — 'Do you want to go?! I always want to go, grandpa!' — and Nick intervenes, breaking it up.
jokes.setup: The party has escalated with older, rougher guests; things turn confrontational
jokes.punchline: 'I always want to go, grandpa' — an enthusiastic, rapid-fire acceptance of the fight challenge — is funny for its eagerness
jokes.explanation: 'Grandpa' as an insult to someone probably not that much older is mildly amusing but the sequence is more dramatic than comedic.

jokes.text: Bill's anti-dominance advice to Sam about Cindy: 'Don't say anything. Be dominant. It's all about dominance. I saw this monkey show on PBS. If you talk to her first, it's a sign of weakness. And she will not pick you to be her mate.'
jokes.setup: Sam panics about what to say to Cindy; Bill offers relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Bill applies primate dominance hierarchy — sourced from a PBS documentary — to teenage dating, including the phrase 'pick you to be her mate'
jokes.explanation: Wonderful Bill moment: his advice is simultaneously scientifically grounded (he watched a nature documentary) and completely inapplicable. 'Pick you to be her mate' is the perfect PBS-vocabulary term for a teenage crush. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Sam kicks Bill out of the room for drinking the NA beer: 'Go into my room, lock the door, and don't drink any more.' Bill: 'That's very dominant.'
jokes.setup: Bill has gotten surprisingly drunk-acting on NA beer; Sam orders him to his room
jokes.punchline: Bill's 'that's very dominant' — assessing Sam's command using their earlier monkey-documentary framework — is a perfect callback delivered from inside his intoxicated haze
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect callback. Bill's own advice is turned against him in the most satisfying way. 'That's very dominant' — three words, maximum payoff — shows Bill is still processing the world through his monkey-documentary lens even while drunk on non-alcoholic beer. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Kim and Daniel are making out in Lindsay's room, having thrown her teddy bears on the floor. Kim, caught: 'Don't worry. We threw your teddy bears on the floor.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay finds Daniel and Kim making out in her bedroom
jokes.punchline: Kim's explanation — offered as reassurance — is that they moved the teddy bears to make space. The implication that this is the considerate part of what they did is absurd.
jokes.explanation: Kim's unironic reassurance about the teddy bears — the only courtesy she could think to extend — is a brilliant character beat. She's not malicious, just completely without social awareness of the violation.

jokes.text: Nick, after attempting to kiss Lindsay, apologizes repeatedly and explains: 'I'm really wasted right now, you know.' Beat. 'John Bonham died.'
jokes.setup: Nick has tried to kiss Lindsay and she's upset; he's profusely apologizing
jokes.punchline: After multiple 'I'm really sorry's, Nick lands on 'John Bonham died' as his final explanatory statement — returning to his grief as the root cause of his behavior
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode — possibly the series. The callback to Nick's opening grief is deployed as an all-purpose explanation for his behavior, which is both hilarious and strangely touching. Nick genuinely believes Bonham's death is connected to everything going wrong tonight. The structural gap — layers of apologies, then one final deflection to a drummer's death — is perfectly constructed. Character integration is near-perfect: no other character could deliver this line.

jokes.text: Neal comforts Lindsay: 'You're smart and pretty. You don't take any crap. And you're the perfect girl, and I've been in love with you since I was 5 years old.'
jokes.setup: Neal is trying to encourage Lindsay by listing her qualities
jokes.punchline: Neal builds to what seems like a pep talk, then lands 'I've been in love with you since I was 5 years old' — an earnest confession that is simultaneously sweet and wildly inappropriate timing
jokes.explanation: The payoff of Neal's crush arc through the episode. The escalation from encouragement to 'I've been in love with you since I was 5' is structurally sound: we feel the warmth and the comedy simultaneously. The '5 years old' specificity is particularly good — it makes the crush feel like a founding document of Neal's identity.

jokes.text: Lindsay calls the police, pretending to be an old neighbor: 'Yes, hello, police? There's a very loud and annoying party going on next door. It's just been going on forever. I'm very tired, and I'm old. I need my rest. I've gotta work tomorrow.'
jokes.setup: Neal suggests Lindsay call the police as a fake neighbor to break up the party
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's performance — adding 'I'm very tired, and I'm old' and 'I've gotta work tomorrow' — over-commits to the bit with a detailed character portrait of a weary neighbor
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's instinct to over-perform the disguise — adding unnecessary old-lady details — is charming character comedy. Her academic intelligence applied to improv has the same earnest overcorrection quality as Neal's Sabbath explanation.

jokes.text: Neal, after the police are called, surveys the chaos: 'I'd say this party has a life expectancy of 5 minutes.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has called the police; the party will soon be over
jokes.punchline: Neal's clinical assessment — 'life expectancy of 5 minutes' — delivered as if diagnosing a patient, is a fun character beat
jokes.explanation: Neal speaking in diagnostic terms about a party is a small, consistent character note. Functional.

jokes.text: Sam's response to Cindy asking where the cute guys are: 'Ha, what? The bus hasn't arrived yet?'
jokes.setup: Cindy has just asked Sam to help her find cute guys at the party Sam is attending
jokes.punchline: Sam's self-deprecating bus quip — implying the cute guys arrive separately and later — is both funny and quietly sad
jokes.explanation: Genuinely excellent line. 'The bus hasn't arrived yet' implies: (a) Sam knows he's not one of the cute guys, (b) he can still be funny about it, (c) it's a slightly melancholy self-awareness that is characteristic of the show's best moments. Strong economy and character integration — this is a Sam joke, delivered in Sam's register. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Police arrive. One of the older, rougher guests panics: 'I'm on parole. Let's get out of here before they search the van.'
jokes.setup: Police arrive at the party; guests scatter
jokes.punchline: The 'I'm on parole' revelation — combined with 'before they search the van' — retroactively contextualizes the older guests as considerably more dangerous than a standard house party implies
jokes.explanation: The escalating implication — parole + suspicious van — delivers a retroactive punch. Contextually funny: these were Daniel's 'friends.'

jokes.text: Sam tries to reassure Ken he doesn't need to run: 'You don't have to run, Ken. It's fake beer.' Ken: 'I know. I won 87 bucks playing quarters. This party ruled.'
jokes.setup: Ken runs when police arrive; Sam explains the beer was fake
jokes.punchline: Ken was running because he won money, not because he was scared of the police — and his review of the party ('this party ruled') is entirely based on the money won while drinking non-alcoholic beer
jokes.explanation: A great misdirection payoff: Ken wasn't scared, he was protecting his winnings. 'This party ruled' as Ken's verdict — based entirely on cash he made at a party with no alcohol — is a perfect character summation. Ken's total self-interest is consistent and satisfying.

jokes.text: Sam's final line as the party ends: 'Should've hired a magician.'
jokes.setup: The party has ended in chaos — police called, teddy bears displaced, Nick making moves on Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Sam's callback to his own earlier suggestion — a magician would have made this a better party — is the perfect final word, because he was right
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. 'Should've hired a magician' is a perfect sitcom button: it rewards everyone who remembered Sam's earnest suggestion, retroactively vindicates him, and summarizes the entire theme of the episode (the geek's instincts were right all along). Five words of maximum structural satisfaction. Economy is near-perfect. The callback earns full points — the gap between the suggestion's context (a kid trying to stop a party) and the delivery's context (aftermath of genuine chaos) is exactly right.

jokes.text: Bill, lying on the ground, moans. Sam: 'Hey, Bill, get up.' Bill: [moans]. Sam: 'That's how the drummer from Led Zeppelin died.'
jokes.setup: Bill is lying on the ground after the party, apparently incapacitated from non-alcoholic beer
jokes.punchline: Sam invokes John Bonham's death — the episode's opening reference — as a threat to get Bill off the floor, implying Bill's condition is comparable to a legendary rock death caused by alcohol poisoning
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed triple-callback closer. John Bonham has been referenced twice — first as Nick's grief, then as his excuse for his behavior — and now Sam uses the same death to motivate Bill off the floor. The fact that Bill has overdone it on non-alcoholic beer makes the comparison even more absurd. Strong economy, strong structure, strong character integration (Sam has absorbed the Bonham lore from the freaks and deployed it in geek-world). One of the episode's best jokes alongside the magician line.

tmdb_overview: Lindsay's friends learn that her parents are going away for the weekend and suggest having a kegger.  Sam and Neal become worried after a school assembly focusing on alcohol-related deaths and they decide to switch the real beer with non-alcoholic beer. Daniel and Kim get back together and despite the lack of alcohol, the party quickly gets out of hand.
